title: Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI
learningObjectives: Explain Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI with phase-5 depth and clarity
Implement Java examples for Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI with production awareness
Identify failure risks and mitigation strategies
Reason about trade-offs under realistic constraints
Design or optimize solutions with maintainability in mind
Present interview answers with concrete evidence and structure

sections.title: Why Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI matters
sections.content:
Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI is a high-value Day 39 topic in Phase 5 (Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns).

At this stage, interviewers evaluate whether you can connect implementation details with reliability, scalability, and maintainability outcomes.

What strong answers include:
1. precise concept framing,
2. operational implications,
3. one realistic failure pattern,
4. one practical mitigation.

This topic is not just about code that compiles. It is about decisions that survive production traffic and team ownership.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI
sections.content:
### Day 39: Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI
Phase context: Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns.

### What to understand deeply
- mechanism and boundaries
- runtime behavior and cost
- operational failure modes
- trade-offs vs alternatives

### Implementation workflow
1. Build a clear baseline solution.
2. Validate edge/failure paths.
3. Measure impact (latency, memory, throughput, or complexity).
4. Refactor for readability and maintainability.

### Interview framing template
- "The problem this solves is..."
- "I chose this approach because..."
- "The key trade-off is..."
- "A failure case is... and mitigation is..."

### Production perspective
For Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI, quality is judged by correctness + resilience + debuggability, not just API usage.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**`@SpringBootApplication`** combines configuration, scanning, autoconfig — `exclude` when auto-config clashes.

**Externalized config:** `application.yml` profiles (`dev`, `prod`) + env vars override — 12-factor readiness.

**Actuator** exposes health/metrics — secure endpoints in production; customize `HealthIndicator` for dependencies.

**Fat JAR** vs layered images — buildpacks optimize Docker layers for dependencies vs code.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Bean with init flag: simulate @PostConstruct ordering in plain Java.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Prototype scope sketch: factory creates new instance each call.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Circular dependency detection: track creating beans in a Set.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 39
sections.description: Visual model for Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Relying on Spring defaults without understanding lifecycle and bean scope.
Leaking transaction boundaries across service methods.
Ignoring lazy loading and query shape in persistence logic.
Implementing security rules without end-to-end flow clarity.
Using framework annotations mechanically without design intent.

sections.title: Exercise - Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI Applied Scenario
sections.problem:
Implement a realistic solution for **Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI**.

Requirements:
1. Define assumptions and constraints first.
2. Include one failure-path handling strategy.
3. Provide one improvement over baseline.
4. State complexity or runtime impact.
5. Keep implementation review-friendly.
hints: Add one observability/debug aid in output or comments.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is Spring bean lifecycle?
conceptual.answer: Instantiate → populate properties → initialize callbacks → use → destroy.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how does **Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI** show up in **production** debugging or **on-call**?
followUps.answer: Tie **logs**, **metrics**, **repro** steps, and **ownership** to what you already said. **Interviewers** reward **symptom → cause → fix → verification**, not textbook **definitions** alone. Instantiate → populate properties → initialize callbacks → use → destroy. Keep **constraints** explicit: **JDK version**, **threading**, **memory**, or **API** contract. Prefer **concrete commands** (`javac`, `java`, `mvn test`) over vague theory. Close with **how you would verify** the answer still holds after a change.

followUps.question: What is a **common trap** or **follow-up** question on **Spring Bean Lifecycle & Advanced DI**?
followUps.answer: Name **one** misconception and **correct** it with a **concrete** check, **command**, or **test**. **Senior** answers stay **specific** and **calm** under pressure. Instantiate → populate properties → initialize callbacks → use → destroy. Separate **compile-time** rules from **runtime** behavior in your explanation. Name **one failure mode** that looks like a bug but is really a misunderstanding. Tie the answer to **interview structure**: clarify, answer, then **one** concrete example.

wrongAnswers: Singleton beans are destroyed after every HTTP request.
BeanPostProcessor is used in every typical business service.
DisposableBean is the only way to run code on shutdown.
@Configuration classes are never subclassed by Spring CGLIB.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 5 Expectation | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Framing | Explain intent + constraints | Start from problem | API-only answer |
| Design | State trade-off explicitly | Compare alternatives | One-solution bias |
| Runtime | Mention cost impact | Time + space/ops | No performance reasoning |
| Failure | Name one incident pattern | Mitigation path | Happy-path only thinking |
| Communication | Be concise + concrete | Use one example | Generic buzzwords |
